primary_diagnosis.name: First-presentation decompensated cirrhosis with new tense ascites, peripheral oedema and mechanical respiratory compromise — presumed chronic hepatitis C aetiology

comorbidities.name: Portal hypertension with oesophageal varices (presumed, at risk)

pathophysiology_summary: Decades of unrecognised chronic hepatitis C (acquired via remote intravenous drug use) has produced progressive hepatic fibrosis and cirrhosis. Distortion of hepatic architecture raises sinusoidal and portal pressure, while failing hepatic synthetic function lowers serum albumin. The combination of portal hypertension and hypoalbuminaemia drives fluid transudation into the peritoneal cavity (tense ascites) and the interstitium (peripheral oedema). The rapidly accumulating ascites splints the diaphragm and, with possible hepatic hydrothorax, produces mechanical respiratory compromise and orthopnoea. This is a first decompensation event: the same portal hypertension threatens variceal haemorrhage, the ascites is at risk of spontaneous bacterial peritonitis, hepatic synthetic failure and precipitants threaten hepatic encephalopathy, and the circulatory disturbance threatens hepatorenal syndrome. Definitive care is inpatient — large-volume paracentesis (with albumin cover), sodium/fluid restriction and titrated diuresis, endoscopic variceal surveillance, and, once stabilised, direct-acting antiviral therapy that can cure the underlying virus.

differential.diagnosis: decompensated chronic liver disease / cirrhosis with ascites
differential.evidence_basis: Rapid tense ascites + peripheral oedema + right upper quadrant discomfort + chronic fatigue in an adult with a remote blood-borne-virus risk factor.

Bilateral leg oedema, breathlessness and orthopnoea overlap with cardiac failure — the classic anchoring trap; distinguished by the hepatic risk history and, on exam, stigmata of chronic liver disease.
New ascites with weight change and cachexia can be malignant; requires imaging and, potentially, ascitic cytology — cannot be excluded remotely.

differential.diagnosis: nephrotic syndrome / renal cause of oedema
differential.evidence_basis: Hypoalbuminaemic oedema and ascites can be renal in origin; separated by urinalysis and bloods.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At intake the AI Doctor should hold decompensated liver disease as leading WHILE keeping cardiac failure and malignancy live; it must not prematurely close on 'just fluid' or on a cardiac cause.

differential.diagnosis: decompensated cirrhosis, presumed chronic hepatitis C
differential.evidence_basis: Remote IVDU + never screened for hepatitis C + chronic fatigue + 'brain fog' + RUQ ache + new tense ascites points strongly to chronic liver disease decompensating.
Still cannot be excluded without examination and investigation; remains a mandatory alternative until cardiac assessment is done.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The IVDU/never-screened history plus the constellation should raise decompensated liver disease to leading, but the correct telehealth stance is 'probable, needs urgent workup', not a confirmed diagnosis.

differential.diagnosis: decompensated cirrhosis with SAAG-high ascites, presumed chronic hepatitis C
differential.evidence_basis: Hospital workup — LFTs, low albumin, deranged coagulation, thrombocytopenia, HCV serology/PCR, liver ultrasound with a coarse/nodular liver and splenomegaly, and a high serum-ascites albumin gradient on diagnostic paracentesis — confirms the picture. Not obtainable via telehealth.

differential_progression.scoring_note: This stage is reachable only in hospital; the AI Doctor's job is to arrange it, not to simulate its results.

differential.diagnosis: first-presentation decompensated cirrhosis, presumed chronic hepatitis C, with tense ascites and respiratory compromise
differential.evidence_basis: Confirmed on inpatient assessment; requires paracentesis, diuresis, variceal surveillance and, once stable, curative antiviral therapy.

red_flags.description: Orthopnoea / mechanical respiratory compromise from tense ascites (± hepatic hydrothorax) — cannot lie flat, tachypnoeic
red_flags.clinical_significance: Fluid splinting the diaphragm impairs ventilation; the immediate reason urgent hospital assessment and drainage are needed.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progressive respiratory failure; a sudden worsening (e.g. large hydrothorax) is an emergency.

red_flags.description: Risk of variceal haemorrhage from portal hypertension (haematemesis / melaena)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Variceal bleeding is a leading cause of death in cirrhosis; currently absent but the risk is high and must be safety-netted.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Catastrophic upper GI haemorrhage and haemorrhagic shock if it occurs and is not escalated immediately.

red_flags.description: Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis in a patient with new ascites
red_flags.clinical_significance: SBP is frequently silent (may lack fever or pain); definitive exclusion needs diagnostic paracentesis, which is a hospital procedure.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated SBP progresses to sepsis, hepatorenal syndrome and death.

red_flags.clinical_significance: A decompensation complication; a new deterioration in consciousness threatens the airway and mandates emergency escalation.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Coma and aspiration if progressive encephalopathy is not recognised and escalated.

red_flags.description: Hepatorenal syndrome / acute kidney injury risk in tense ascites with circulatory compromise
red_flags.clinical_significance: Effective hypovolaemia and splanchnic vasodilatation threaten renal perfusion; recognised only on bloods (creatinine) — clinician/laboratory finding.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Rapidly progressive renal failure with high mortality if not managed in hospital.

red_flags.description: Rapid massive fluid accumulation causing functional collapse (bedbound, cannot mobilise, fall risk)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Marks acute decompensation and drives the respiratory compromise; also a falls/immobility hazard.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delay in recognising decompensation; falls and injury from unsupported mobilisation.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Anchoring on right-sided heart failure for the bilateral oedema, breathlessness and orthopnoea, and missing the hepatic origin signalled by the risk history.
Attributing the long-standing fatigue and 'brain fog' to normal ageing, so the chronic liver disease (and possible early encephalopathy) is never considered.
Treating this as 'just bloating / fluid / weight gain' and giving reassurance or a routine GP referral instead of recognising acute decompensation and its lethal complications.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Failure to elicit the blood-borne-virus risk
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Not sensitively asking about remote injecting drug use / hepatitis C screening, so the aetiology and the whole diagnostic thread are missed.
Assuming spontaneous bacterial peritonitis is excluded because the patient is afebrile — SBP is often silent and needs a diagnostic tap.
Attempting to initiate or titrate diuretics, or manage tense ascites, remotely — instead of escalating to urgent hospital assessment for paracentesis, bloods and imaging.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Likely Child-Pugh B/C once bloods available (ascites present; albumin/bilirubin/INR pending)
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Advanced hepatic dysfunction; the components (albumin, bilirubin, INR) require bloods and cannot be scored via telehealth.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Cannot be computed remotely — requires bilirubin, INR, creatinine and sodium
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Prognostic severity and transplant-listing metric; a laboratory score, named here only to underline that the AI cannot generate it.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Abdominal examination — shifting dullness, fluid thrill, tenderness, tender hepatomegaly
Stigmata of chronic liver disease — spider naevi, palmar erythema, proximal muscle wasting
Clinician-measured blood pressure, heart rate, respiratory rate and oxygen saturation
Diagnostic paracentesis and ascitic fluid analysis to exclude spontaneous bacterial peritonitis and measure the SAAG
Bloods — LFTs, albumin, bilirubin, FBC/platelets, coagulation/INR, urea and electrolytes, creatinine, ammonia
Hepatitis C serology and HCV RNA PCR
Liver ultrasound (and Doppler) and any cross-sectional imaging
Upper GI endoscopy for oesophageal varices
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise probable decompensated liver disease with respiratory compromise and arrange urgent same-day hospital assessment/admission (ambulance transport) for diagnostic paracentesis, bloods, imaging and diuresis; give conservative interim advice the patient can act on (sit upright with legs elevated, sip fluids, avoid added salt) and explicitly safety-net the emergencies (haematemesis, melaena, new confusion, sudden severe breathlessness) with a 000 instruction.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Cirrhosis and hepatitis C cannot be confirmed remotely — the real risk is UNDER-action (managing at home, routine referral, or false reassurance) or anchoring on a cardiac cause, rather than over-calling the diagnosis.

guidelines_referenced.reference: EASL Clinical Practice Guidelines for the management of patients with decompensated cirrhosis (2018)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: New-onset grade 2–3 ascites warrants diagnostic paracentesis, sodium restriction and stepped diuretic therapy; tense ascites with respiratory compromise warrants therapeutic large-volume paracentesis with albumin.

guidelines_referenced.reference: AASLD Practice Guidance: Diagnosis, Evaluation and Management of Ascites (2021)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: First-onset ascites requires diagnostic paracentesis to determine SAAG and exclude spontaneous bacterial peritonitis before or at the start of treatment.
